sections.heading: Birth delivery improved nationwide, but some places still lag behind
sections.body: Among live births and stillbirths combined, 93.5 percent were delivered by a skilled provider in 2025, and 93.5 percent were delivered in a health facility. Both improved from 2022, when skilled attendance was 89.6 percent and facility delivery was 88.5 percent. Caesarean delivery also increased, from 19.5 percent to 21.9 percent, but this should be read as a pattern in how births are delivered, not simply as proof of better access. Overall, delivery care improved widely, so the key question is now where care is still much less complete.

sections.heading: BARMM improved a lot but still has the largest regional gap
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest 2025 levels for both skilled attendance and facility delivery, at 69.3 percent and 68.6 percent. These were much higher than its 2022 levels of 42.4 percent for skilled attendance and 39.1 percent for facility delivery. Even with that large improvement, BARMM was still more than 24 points below the national level on each measure. This makes BARMM both the biggest progress story and the clearest remaining access gap in the regional table.

sections.heading: Some regions were already close to full delivery-care coverage
sections.body: Several regions had skilled attendance and facility delivery at or near universal levels in 2025. CALABARZON reported 99.5 percent for both measures. Central Visayas had 99.0 percent skilled attendance and 99.1 percent facility delivery, while CAR had 99.3 percent skilled attendance and 98.7 percent facility delivery. These regions were not only above the national average; they had very little room left for further gains on the core delivery-care measures. Their contrast with BARMM and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ shows that the national average hides a wide regional spread.

sections.heading: Zamboanga Peninsula stayed low and barely changed from 2022
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ recorded 76.1 percent skilled attendance and 75.5 percent facility delivery in 2025. Both were almost the same as in 2022, when the region reported 75.6 percent skilled attendance and 76.0 percent facility delivery. This is different from BARMM, where levels stayed low but improved substantially. Zamboanga Peninsula therefore stands out less as a fast-improving region and more as a place where delivery-care coverage remained persistently low.

sections.heading: Caesarean delivery varied widely by region
sections.body: In 2025, the caesarean section share ranged from 7.5 percent in NIR and 7.6 percent in BARMM to 38.2 percent in Central Luzon and 32.7 percent in NCR. Some regions with very high skilled attendance and facility delivery had much lower caesarean shares, including Central Visayas at 10.8 percent and Caraga at 12.0 percent. This means caesarean delivery does not simply rise and fall with the two access measures. It may reflect differences in delivery practice, case mix, referral patterns, or service availability, which the table can flag but cannot explain by itself.

sections.heading: For younger mothers, the bigger difference is caesarean delivery
sections.body: Mothers under age 20 had 90.3 percent skilled attendance and 90.7 percent facility delivery in 2025. Both were below the national level but still relatively high. The bigger age difference was in caesarean delivery: 11.6 percent among mothers under 20, compared with 21.0 percent among mothers aged 20 to 34 and 29.5 percent among mothers aged 35 to 49. Skilled attendance and facility delivery improved across age groups from 2022 to 2025, while caesarean delivery among mothers under 20 fell from 14.6 percent. Age therefore matters more for the type of delivery than for whether births happen with skilled or facility-based care.

sections.heading: The urban-rural gap narrowed because rural facility delivery rose faster
sections.body: Urban births still had higher coverage than rural births in 2025. Skilled-provider delivery was 95.9 percent in urban areas versus 91.3 percent in rural areas, and facility delivery was 95.5 percent versus 91.9 percent. Since 2022, rural gains were larger, especially for facility delivery, which rose from 85.0 percent to 91.9 percent. Urban facility delivery also increased, but by a smaller amount, from 91.8 percent to 95.5 percent. The residence gap still exists, but recent progress was stronger in rural areas.

sections.heading: The biggest education gap is among mothers with no education
sections.body: Mothers with no education had 59.2 percent skilled attendance and 54.2 percent facility delivery in 2025, far below every other education group. Mothers with Grades 1 to 6 were also below the national level, at 80.9 percent skilled attendance and 80.0 percent facility delivery, but their levels were much higher than those of mothers with no education. At the other end, college-educated mothers had 98.5 percent skilled attendance and 98.4 percent facility delivery. This means the education pattern is not just a steady ladder; the lowest education group is separated by a much larger gap.

sections.heading: Wealth gaps stayed large even after gains among the poorest households
sections.body: In the lowest wealth quintile, 82.9 percent of births were delivered by a skilled provider and 81.6 percent were delivered in a health facility in 2025. These were large gains from 2022, when the figures were 72.7 percent and 71.2 percent. However, the highest wealth quintile was still near universal, with 99.2 percent skilled attendance and 99.7 percent facility delivery. Caesarean delivery showed an even wider wealth gap, from 9.3 percent in the lowest quintile to 50.8 percent in the highest.

takeaways: Birth delivery care improved nationwide from 2022 to 2025, with both skilled attendance and facility delivery reaching 93.5 percent.
BARMM improved a lot, but in 2025 it was still the lowest region for both skilled attendance and facility delivery.
Zamboanga Peninsula¹ changed little from 2022 and stayed well below the national level on the two core delivery-care measures.

Caesarean delivery varies sharply by wealth and across regions, and it does not simply track skilled attendance or facility delivery. It should be read as a delivery-pattern indicator, not as a simple access score.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
